title: Iran Oil Waivers Open, BOJ Hits 1%, Starmer Falls, China Hits Rare Earths
subhead: The US-Iran deal grants Tehran immediate oil sales waivers for 60 days while keeping broader sanctions intact, the Bank of Japan raises rates to a 31-year high, Keir Starmer resigns as UK prime minister, and China targets US rare earth firms in an escalating trade war.

sections.heading: US-Iran Deal: Oil Waivers Now, Everything Else Locked
sections.body:
The memorandum of understanding signed by Presidents Trump and Pezeshkian on June 17 grants Iran immediate waivers to sell crude oil freely during a 60-day negotiation window, while the broader UN, US, and EU sanctions architecture remains fully intact, according to Crypto Briefing's analysis of the deal. Iran has pledged to dilute its stockpile of highly enriched uranium; in return, the US committed to not imposing additional sanctions during the negotiation period. Existing sanctions stay.

The deal also commits both sides to reopening the Strait of Hormuz, which had been effectively blocked during the conflict. Iran's parliamentary speaker Ghalibaf said Hormuz will 'never return to pre-war operations' and announced a new Iranian management regime for the waterway, including a telephone hotline, News24 reported. The largest tanker operator Frontline cautioned that full Hormuz flows could take 'weeks or a month' to resume, according to LiveMint.

Iran is simultaneously running what AOL described as an 'insurance racket' on Hormuz shipping — requiring vessels to purchase Iranian insurance at inflated rates as a condition of transit. The US Treasury sanctioned Iranian crypto exchange Nobitex on June 2, just two weeks before the MOU was signed, showing that enforcement against Iranian financial infrastructure continued even as diplomatic channels were active.

Brent crude fell below $80 a barrel on Monday as negotiators agreed on a roadmap to a final deal, according to Business Times Singapore. Oil had traded down $1.55 the prior session on easing tensions, per Daily News.
sections.whyItMatters: This is a narrow carve-out, not a sanctions lift. Iran can sell oil but its banks remain locked out of SWIFT, its entities stay blacklisted, and the entire arrangement expires in August if negotiations collapse. The oil waiver removes upside risk from crude prices but the Hormuz insurance racket and Ghalibaf's rhetoric signal Iran intends to maintain leverage over shipping corridors regardless of the deal.

sections.heading: Bank of Japan Raises Rate to 1%, Highest Since 1995
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark policy rate from 0.75% to 1.0% at its two-day monetary policy meeting ending June 17, the highest level since September 1995, according to AP News and UPI. The decision was approved by a 7-1 vote; Governor Kazuo Ueda was hospitalised and did not attend or vote.

The BOJ cited rising oil prices from Middle East tensions and the need to achieve its 2% inflation target sustainably. The yen initially fell to a 23-month low of 160.80 against the dollar — markets had largely priced in the hike. Major Japanese banks (MUFG, SMBC, Mizuho) raised ordinary deposit rates from 0.3% to 0.4% effective August 3, still a fraction of the new policy rate.

Mizuho Research estimated that additional interest income on deposits could exceed higher borrowing costs by roughly 1 trillion yen ($6.3 billion) annually across households — but the gains concentrate among older savers, while 80% of Japanese mortgage borrowers use variable-rate loans and face higher payments. Monthly payments on a 50 million yen variable-rate mortgage would rise by about 5,900 yen ($37). Recurring profits across non-financial industries could decline about 1%, with companies capitalised below 10 million yen facing a 6.6% profit drop.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is exiting its ultra-low-rate era, and the distributional effects are stark: older savers gain, younger borrowers and small businesses lose. The yen's continued weakness despite the hike shows markets doubt the BOJ's resolve to keep tightening. Sony's return to the dollar bond market after 28 years (see below) is a direct consequence.

sections.heading: Sony Returns to Dollar Bond Market After 28 Years
sections.body:
Sony Group mandated Bank of America and Morgan Stanley to hold investor calls starting June 22 for its first US dollar bond sale since 1998, when it raised $1.5 billion, Bloomberg reported via Business Times Singapore. A two-tranche offering with maturities of five and 10 years is expected, rated A2 by Moody's and A+ by S&P.

The move is a direct response to the Bank of Japan's rate hikes making yen-denominated borrowing more expensive. Japanese companies have been selling record amounts of euro-denominated notes as rate differentials narrow, and the dollar market is now attractive again as Sony diversifies its funding base. Denso sold $500 million in investment-grade dollar notes on Monday, and Mitsubishi raised $1 billion earlier in June. Toyota priced 1 billion euros of debt on Monday in the eurobond market.

Sony last year spun off its insurance and banking arm to focus on entertainment — games, music, and film. S&P upgraded Sony to A+ in March, citing strong earnings and cash flow outlook. The offering is part of a broader rush into US high-grade bonds as companies lock in tight credit spreads before expected Fed rate hikes, with SpaceX also preparing a $20+ billion offering.
sections.whyItMatters: Sony's 28-year absence from the dollar market ending is a signal: Japanese corporates are restructuring their funding as domestic rates rise and yen weakness makes foreign-currency debt cheaper to service. Expect more Japanese issuers to follow.

sections.heading: ECB's Lagarde: No Need for 'More Forceful' Response Despite War Inflation
sections.body:
ECB President Christine Lagarde told European lawmakers in Brussels on June 22 that the ECB sees no evidence yet of de-anchoring of inflation expectations or second-round effects that would warrant a more forceful policy response, according to Business Times Singapore. She acknowledged some de-anchoring in short-term inflation expectations but said longer-term views remain 'broadly unchanged'.

The ECB currently holds its deposit rate at 2%, with core inflation accelerating to 2.6% in May from 2.2% in April — even stronger than initially reported. Markets priced 33 basis points of additional tightening by year-end, down from 37 before Lagarde spoke, implying at least one more quarter-point hike to 2.5%. German two-year yields fell six basis points to 2.59% on her comments.

Lagarde welcomed last week's US-Iran pact but stressed the 'continuing fragility' of the situation. She said the ECB is now positioned 'somewhere between our baseline and mild scenarios' for the economy and must remain agile. Spain's ECB board member Jose Luis Escriva said the bank must be 'vigilant' about second-round effects on wages.
sections.whyItMatters: Lagarde is walking a tightrope: inflation above target and rising core prices, but no wage-price spiral yet. She's deliberately holding back from a hawkish pivot while keeping the door open — the oil waiver and Hormuz reopening ease the energy shock, but the ECB still has at least one more hike priced in.

sections.heading: Starmer Resigns: UK Political Shock, Markets Cautious
sections.body:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer resigned on Monday, less than two years after leading Labour to a landslide 411-seat victory in July 2024, AP News reported. His premiership unravelled amid a struggling economy, multiple policy U-turns — on winter heating subsidies, welfare cuts, and agricultural inheritance tax — and the disastrous appointment of Peter Mandelson as US ambassador, which backfired after revelations about Mandelson's ties to Jeffrey Epstein.

Andy Burnham, the former Greater Manchester mayor, is the likely next prime minister after Wes Streeting declined to contest the leadership, according to the Khaleej Times. Burnham has positioned himself as a decentralising, place-first politician who wants to shift power from London, but his critics note he has frequently changed policy positions. He recently moved toward the centre ground, saying the nation's finances are too constrained for large-scale nationalisations and that Britain will not rejoin the EU anytime soon.

Bloomberg's opinion desk noted that bond markets are sceptical of Burnham, with the piece titled 'The Bond Market's Skepticism of Burnham Is a Warning.' The pound held lower against the dollar following Starmer's resignation, Reuters reported. Burnham has previously criticised the government for being 'in hock to the bond markets' — comments that gilt traders are now scrutinising closely.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK has had seven prime ministers in a decade. Burnham's instinct toward fiscal expansion and nationalisation will collide with gilt market discipline — his own words about bond markets will be thrown back at him. The pound's muted reaction so far reflects uncertainty, not complacency.

sections.heading: China Targets US Rare Earth Firms as Trade War Escalates
sections.body:
Beijing imposed export controls on 10 US entities including rare earth producers MP Materials and USA Rare Earth, along with defense contractors and drone manufacturers, the Washington Post and Reuters reported. The controls prohibit Chinese companies from supplying dual-use items to these firms, and third-country actors are also banned from transferring China-origin dual-use goods to them.

China also barred 46 US companies from government procurement, including Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, and Boeing's defense division. The measures retaliate for the Pentagon's expansion of its 'Chinese Military Companies' list, which recently added Alibaba, Baidu, Tencent, CATL, DJI, Huawei, and Hikvision — Baidu called the designation 'totally baseless'.

The rare earth dimension is sharpening: China's rare-earth magnet exports to Japan fell 34.6% month-on-month in May to 123 metric tons, UPI reported, with monthly shipments below 200 tons since March. Tungsten carbide exports to Japan registered zero for four consecutive months from February. Japan's TDK said in February that China's controls had made material procurement increasingly difficult.

MP Materials operates the only active rare earth mine in the United States, and China controls roughly 70% of global processing capacity. The Strait Times described Beijing's move as a 'calibrated response' to the Pentagon blacklist, signalling continued superpower competition despite stabilising diplomatic ties.
sections.whyItMatters: China is proving it can weaponise critical mineral supply chains with surgical precision — not blanket bans, but targeted licensing delays that squeeze specific countries and firms. Japan's tungsten carbide supply has been at zero for four months. The US rare earth mine is now directly in the crosshairs.

sections.heading: Canada Holds Firm on Russia Shadow Fleet Sanctions as US Pauses
sections.body:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney announced new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, energy revenues, defense-industrial sector, and disinformation entities on June 16, Reuters reported. The move stands in contrast to the Trump administration's decision to pause sanctions on Russian shadow fleet vessels, which CBC Canada reported was made at Washington's behest.

According to CBC, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz stated that all G7 members except the US opposed the pause on shadow fleet sanctions. The split highlights a growing transatlantic divergence on Russia policy: Canada and European allies are maintaining pressure on Moscow's maritime sanctions-busting network while the US has eased enforcement.

The shadow fleet — aging, uninsured tankers Russia uses to move oil above the $60 price cap — has been a central enforcement target since 2023. CBC's March 13 report first revealed the US pause. Carney's new sanctions package signals that Ottawa intends to fill at least part of the enforcement gap the American retreat has created.
sections.whyItMatters: A G7 split on Russian sanctions enforcement is significant. The shadow fleet is the mechanism by which Russia funds its war economy; if the US steps back while allies hold the line, enforcement becomes uneven and Russian oil continues to flow — just through more circuitous routes.

bottomLine: June 23 marks the day the US-Iran deal's fine print became clear: oil waivers are real but narrow, Hormuz stays under Iranian leverage, and broader sanctions remain locked down. Meanwhile, the BOJ's 1% rate — the highest in 31 years — is pushing Japanese corporates like Sony toward dollar funding, and the ECB is navigating between war-driven inflation and Lagarde's insistence that second-round effects haven't materialised. Starmer's resignation introduces UK political risk just as gilt markets eye Burnham's fiscal instincts warily. And China's surgical targeting of US rare earth producers and Japanese supply chains shows Beijing can calibrate economic pain without resorting to tariffs.
sources: AP News, UPI, Business Times Singapore, Crypto Briefing, News24, LiveMint, AOL, Washington Post, CBC Canada, Reuters, Bloomberg, Khaleej Times, Nikkei Asia, Financial Express India, Daily News, Strait Times
